parentQuestion: An elderly patient presents with double vision when looking downward (especially...

scaffolds.q: Each extraocular muscle has a primary action (direction of pull).
scaffolds.explain: The six extraocular muscles each have specific primary actions: superior rectus (up), inferior rectus (down), medial rectus (in), lateral rectus (out), superior oblique (down + intorsion), inferior oblique (up + extorsion). Allow your mind to embrace this:the mechanical elegance — six cables, six directions, coordinated three-dimensional control.

scaffolds.q: Patients often develop compensatory head positions to minimize double vision.
scaffolds.explain: When one eye muscle fails, the brain receives mismatched images (diplopia). patients intuitively tilt or turn their head to a position where the weak muscle doesn't need to work. This minimizes double vision. The body compensates. Beautiful adaptation.
